sentences.text: Having lived on the border, the shooting went on already for weeks.
Just before the war broke out, Germany expelled all the Jews of Polish origin.
They put them on the border.
The Poles would not let them in.
The Poles said they left-- lived in Germany for so many years, they're not their responsibility.
Germans said, they were not born in Germany.
They're not Germans.
And this is what I remember of 1939, when all these Jewish families were sitting on the border in no man's land.
And my mother was going-- collecting blankets, umbrellas, rain coats, and was running there with food.
The Jewish women in the area were cooking and collecting food and bringing it to these people who sat outside for days, because the Poles would not let them in.
Somehow there was some international intervention, because finally the Poles would let them in.
And so they went into Poland.
It was tragic, because these-- many of them were born in Germany, but their parents came from Poland.
That was--
We're talking July.
End of June, beginning of July.
Then I remember the shooting started.
Day and night we heard shooting.
But nobody crossed the border.
There was a lot of rhetoric.
The Poles said they're going to chase them out, and they're going to win the war.
On September 1, at 12:00 noon, there was a special announcement that the president of Poland will speak.
We were the only ones with a radio, and so people were gathering outside.
My father opened the window and had the radio full blast, and people were listening to the Polish president declaring war on Germany.
Within one hour, you could see people starting to move.
Immediately they were packing up on their little buggies.
There-- they put their old grandparents on the buggies, covered them with-- with blankets, tied the goats to the buggies, and schlepped them.
It was an exodus of people, and it was such a sad picture to see.
They were leaving.
They didn't know where they're going, but they're running away from the border, because they thought that this is where the fight-- that that's where the war is going to be.
So they left everything behind, just took some food and clothing and some animals with them, and just left towards Poland, inside Poland.
My grandmother phoned up that she's leaving, too.
And I remember my mother said, stay where you are.
Don't go anywhere.
It's too late to go.
I know the German strength.
I saw what was happening in Berlin.
And I know that the Poles have not gotten a chance.
And you'll see that, in no time, Germany will overrun all of Poland.
But she wanted to-- us to be safe, so she sent my sister and myself to some friends, which I don't even know-- I didn't even know her name, their names.
She gave us the address.

We got on the bicycle-- it was about 30 kilometers from our town-- and we put on our Girl Guide uniform-- till this day I don't understand why-- and we went, showing, probably, the-- our allegiance to Poland.

I have no idea what made us do this, but we were lucky that we weren't killed because of that, because when the Nazis marched into Katowice, which was 20 kilometers from us, a Girl Guide in her uniform approached a German officer, presented him with flowers, welcomed him to Poland, and shot him.
And they were looking for everyone that was in that organization.
Now, remember, scouts are not a violent organization.
And this girl wore-- wore a Girl Guide uniform.
And we-- my sister and I, like idiots-- are running away in those Girl Guide uniforms.
We were lucky [CHUCKLES] that we weren't caught and killed.
We were going near.
It was in a little town.
I don't even remember the name of this place.
But a day later, my mother phoned.
We should come back, because it's all-- so we were ready-- the German soldiers already occupied that little town.
We just get on our bikes, put on regular clothes, and went back home.
When we came home, our store was closed.
My father was not home.
And my mother told me that she's hiding him in-- at the-- in the neighbor's place, because she was afraid that they will take him.
And then--
The Nazis.
The Nazis.
We were-- we were the first ones.
In two hours, the town was occupied.
It was no-- no shot fired.
They just came in.
There was no shot fired.
They just marched in, that was it.
And then there was many Germans lived there that immediately put on their uniforms and joined them, because there were Germans and Poles living there.
So they just put on their uniforms.
The swastikas were all over the place.
All flags flying from windows.
People that you thought where your friends, suddenly their swastikas were flying out of their windows.
And there was an amazing change.
The next day, it was-- they came.
They broke into our store, took out all the merchandise.
The people helped themselves.
It was such a commotion, and there was nothing my mother could do.
Just stay away, and stay out of the way.
I walked down the street.
We were not allowed to speak to any of the non-Jewish people.
I'm 12-years-old, the only friends I have are non-Jewish.
There are no Jews, and suddenly I don't have a friend.
No one speaks to me.
They're not allowed, and I'm not allowed to speak to them.
I asked, why?
And I'm still asking why.
Why am I different today?
Who am I today?
I think I'm the same I was yesterday.
And on that day, my dream of becoming a doctor, going to school, was shattered.
My life changed in one hour.
My father couldn't forgive himself for not leaving.
My mother was pressuring him so much to go.
For years, whenever she went to Berlin to visit her sister-- and she was there very often-- she came home and she always said, it's going to be too late.
Let's leave.
Where am I going to go with two grown-up daughters?
How I'm going to provide for them?
How I'm going to marry them off one day?
How am I going to make a living?
Where am I going to go?
And the Poles will not allow you to take anything with you.
The moment you would have sold your business or your house, the Polish brigade-- a special brigade-- was immediately there.
Your passport was confiscated.
You could not leave Poland.
So my father just-- he didn't have the courage to leave.
My mother said, let's go.
And then he couldn't forgive himself.
He couldn't forgive himself.
He aged in the two, three days.
Like, it was unbelievable.
And then they started to take him every day.
First they came, they picked him.
They picked him up, and we didn't know if we were going to see him again.
But he came back at night.
They picked up the Poles, and him, and they marched him through the town with all kind of signs.
I-- I am the Jew that cheated you all my life.
And they just tried to humiliate him in the community where he grew up-- and truly didn't grow up, but lived there all since his adult-- since the the World War I.
And my grandfather as well.
And they had them work, sweep the streets, the sidewalks.
They pick him up every day, and we never knew if he'll come back.
Sometimes they beat him up.
And we would wait all day, and hope and pray that he would come back.
No, no.
No, not at that time.
This is when we start-- had to wear-- that's when we had to wear the Star of David.
But at that time, it was an arm band.
It was a white arm band with a blue Star of David on it.
And you could not leave your home without it.
Jews were not allowed to do their shopping during the day.
They had-- they were only allowed in the evening.
By that time-- remember, this is war-- there was no merchandise left.
So we were on the mercy of our nice neighbors that would go shopping and bring us the food.
Now, our bank account was frozen.
We were not allowed to withdraw any money.
Jews were not allowed to withdraw any funds.
We had no income, because no store.
Jews had to deliver, to the police station, all silver and gold.
We had little earrings that grandmother gave us when we were a year old.
Even this we had to give.
Wedding bands, anything in jewelry, any silver, candlesticks, any silver that was in the house, under threat of death, had to be delivered to the police.
Oh, yes.
They meticulously made a list of what you gave them, and you got the piece of paper that you had delivered all that to them.
Jews were not allowed to wear any fur coats.
And they would confiscate even coats with fur collars.
This is the life that we led at that time.
They all-- they all went through the same ordeal that we did.
So Upper Silesia became part of Germany proper.
It became German Reich again.
And on October 19, 1939, they came and picked up my father.
They gave him a few hours warning--
The Nazis.
And that day I won't forget either. 1939, October 19.
Every year I remember.

My mother was running around when he was ready to go, and had him put on another shirt, another sweater, and one more sweater, because winter is coming, and I don't know where you're going to be and what-- when we will see you again.
At least you'll have clothes, because they weren't allowed to take anything with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And take another scarf.
And then they came.
And all I remember is he turned to me and he said, you take care of mother while I'm gone.
I had just-- I had just been 14-years-old.
It was a week after my birthday.
And at 14, now I was no longer a child, because now I had the responsibility to care of my mother.
--they took all--
They took all men, from 16 to 65, from Upper Silesia and shipped them away.
And nobody knew where they went.
My mother went every day to the city, to Katowice, to Konig city-- which now was changed again, the names, into German-- where more Jews were living, and was searching for some news.
Maybe somebody came back.
Maybe somebody brought some news.
Nobody-- those-- these men disappeared.
I cannot describe the nights that I sat with my mother crying, praying, wondering what's going to happen and what's going to be and how long we're going to stay.
A terrible thing happened.
One day, she-- we still had-- she had some money, and Jews were not allowed to have any money.
So she packed it all and put it-- in Europe you had an oven-- like we have now electric, you had one that you made a fire to bake your bread in the kitchen.
And she hid it-- hid the money in the oven.
And in the whole commotion in being so upset and distraught as she was, she wanted to bake some bread, since we couldn't buy anything.
And she made fire in the oven, and suddenly remembered that all her money was there.
Everything we had was there in flames.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She took it out, as burned as it was that little pack-- package with the money, the bill-- paper bills.
But she had her mind in the right place.
She wrapped it up in cotton, and she sent it to Berlin in the national German bank with the name Steinitz, Alfreda Steinitz.
They had no idea that they were dealing with a Jew.
She wrote them that she inadvertently burned the money.
There was a fire in the house.
They bought the story, and they sent us back the money.
They gave us-- they could see the numbers, and they sent back the money.
They didn't know that they were sending the money to-- to Jews.
But that was my mother.
That's how she acted.
I think I inherited some of this from her.
So here we were, not knowing what happened to the men.
And somewhere in February, somebody came back, and my mother found out what happened.
They took the men to Polish/Russian border, and they shot them in the forest.
Like they couldn't shoot them in the forest right there.
They had to take them right across Poland.
Some of the men jump out from those train from the wagons, and ran.
And this one man that came back, a young man, was one of them.
And he said some of them saved themselves.
The rest, they shot.
We had no idea.
No.
No idea.
It was end of February when we got a postcard from Russia.
He was one of the few, because he was so athletic.
He jumped, he ran, saved himself, ran across the boarder, and he sent us a postcard from Russia, which was once Poland.
Lemberg was Poland.
That's where he was.
But he was alive.
Well, at least he was alive.
I remember my mother sitting in the kitchen and saying, I would like to have him back just for one year.
If I could only have him back-- this was such a happy marriage-- for one year.
Well, March 3, we got an order that they will make Upper Silesia judenrein, and that we all have to leave.
And so the day came.
We were allowed 20 kilo to take with us.
That's a day I won't remember-- won't forget easily, either.
How can you forget?
Mother was walking around in the house, what do I take?
Do I take some pots and pans and utensils?
Some pillows and blankets?
Clothes?
Do I take my pictures of my family?
My parents, grandparents?
Do I take the tablecloth that my grandmother embroidered for my wedding?
What do I take with me when I am leaving my home and don't know if I'll ever be back?
Well, at 14, in those days, you were a child.
I still had hopes that maybe the war will end, and I go back to school.
I took my school books with me and my doll and my teddy bear.
My biggest problem was the dog.
I had Schpitz, a white little dog.
And I didn't know what I'm going to do with my dog.
If I leave him inside, he'll starve.
If I leave him outside, he'll freeze.
March, in Poland, is still cold.
This was my big problem.
Mother had other problems.
And I remember, when we were ready to leave-- because they told us where to report to-- and there was no point in running away.
Everybody knew us.
It wasn't like you lived in a ghetto and you weren't even registered.
Everybody knew us in the whole radius around.
I never needed an identification when I crossed the border.
I was Josef Steinitz's daughter.
And I will cross the border.
Everywhere I went, I was Josef Steinitz's daughter.
I couldn't run away.
You couldn't hide.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And as we are leaving, my mother stands at the door with the key.
Should I lock the door?
What's the point?
There's no point of locking the door.
I'll never see anything that I'm leaving behind.
And we left.
Were there any of your neighbors in the streets watching?
No.
Everybody stayed behind closed doors.
Nobody had the courage to come out and even ask.
But then, in that climate, you have to also think of where they were and where we were.
I don't know if we, today, would have the courage to stand up for a minority if we would be in a similar situation.
That's why the righteous that saved the Jews have-- can never be forgotten, because we must think of them in the climate and in the times that we were living.
Isolated.
I wore the band, the-- that Star of David.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't even know if I was embarrassed wearing it or not, or if I was proud to wear it.
It's just something that they forced me to do, and I could not walk out the house without it.
No.
We were supposed to meet.
As a matter of-- strangely enough, we met in Rabbi Plaut's uncle's house, and-- who was my mother's teacher when she was--
--in school.
The town's name was Szymanowice.
A little town.
As I said, there's so many little towns around.
And there were about 35 families that gathered there, and we were picked up and loaded on trucks.
And from there, we left.
It was already evening when we left.
And we arrived in a town-- didn't know the name of it-- after a few hours of driving.
And were unload-- got off the trucks on the town square.
35 families.
Very few children.
Almost none.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we found out the next day where we were.
We were left in the middle of the night, and we didn't know where to go.
In the morning, we could see people looking out the windows and behind the curtains.
And then they're starting to come and talk to us.
But I didn't understand the language.
This is the first time in my life I heard Yiddish.
And they all looked so strange to me, because they had sidelocks.
And they wear those black coats and hats.
And they had beards.
And I don't know who they were.
I was hiding behind my mother.
I didn't know they were Jews.
I never saw Hasidic or Polish Jews, because my whole life was on the other side of the border.
Then we found out that we are in a town called Jaworzno.
J-A-W-O-R-Z-N-O.
It was Poland, near Krakow.
It was also a mining town.
And now it was a ghetto.
Already Jews from the surrounding areas were herded into the few square blocks.
And of course, it wasn't like Canada.
People did not live in homes-- four-bedroom homes.
If they had a room and a kitchen and a bedroom, the whole family lived there.
Sometimes the grandparents, and they slept in the kitchen.
And now they brought all these people from around the town, because the Jews could not live outside of the town.
And now the 35 families arrived.
But Jews demonstrate that they take care of Jews.
We do take care of each other.
And so there was a school-- a Jewish school building that was not used during the Nazi occupation.
And so they put us up in that school building.
And everybody brought us something.
People brought us some dishes and cutlery and pots.
And somehow we set up a home.
We had one room.
And everybody thought the war will end in six months.
And now that since we knew that our father is safe, so we thought that we'll stay there and-- and we will be reunited one day.
My mother desperately tried to get a visa to go to the Soviet Union.
She even wrote to the embassy in Berlin.
But there was no way that the Soviets would let anyone in.
But this is-- she always took these initiatives.
And there was no stopping her.
And she even went to Berlin-- took off her arm band, and nobody would know that she's Jewish, with the fluent German-- and went to the embassy herself.
Well, they took all the information, but we never heard from them.
So this failed.
We couldn't go and be reunited.
And somehow, we didn't have the courage to go on our own.
Just-- I guess she didn't want to endanger us.
She would have gone, but she couldn't leave us behind, my sister and I.
Well, it's about 80 kilometers.
No, because I remember Jaworzno-- passing by there when we went with my father to Krakow-- was a 90-kilometer bicycle ride before the war.
And I remember stopping at the coal mine.
We had our lunch outside, because there was such nice grass.
And that's what is so ironical, that in that town we had our lunch on the way-- on our beautiful trip, with beautiful memories.
And now we were brought here into the ghetto.
And we stayed there and were hoping that we'll be there.
But unfortunately, things got very bad.
First of all, you could not get a ration, like food, unless you worked.
So we would work for the farmers doing-- you know, in-- in the fields.
And then we would get some potatoes, so we would work for food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Basically, you tried not to be outside if you didn't have to.
The fear of the German-- the Nazis, when they walked the streets, you just did not want to meet them.
You did your shopping-- if you got your-- your-- your little ration cards, and you went round to get the shopping.
And you basically stayed in the house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you were waiting for the war to end.
